sections.title: How is Waste Created?
sections.content_summary: Everyday human activities generate a wide variety of waste — old clothes, tins, bottles, paper, vegetable and fruit peels, plastic wrappers, pencil shavings, old batteries and more. When not managed properly, waste accumulates in garbage piles around homes, schools and neighbourhoods. The section invites children to observe their own surroundings, recall waste-producing activities, and begin thinking about what happens to waste.

tasks.task_text: What are the children and the teacher doing in the picture?
Why are they cleaning the park?
Have you ever seen piles of waste lying around your home or school?
Have you ever thought about how this waste is created?
Recall different activities that you and your elders do during the day. How do these activities create waste? Finally, what do we do with the waste? Share your findings with your classmates.

sections.title: Ways to Manage Waste
sections.content_summary: Unmanaged waste causes serious harm: animals die from eating plastic, burning plastic releases harmful gases, wastewater breeds mosquitoes and spreads disease. Yet some communities have transformed their surroundings through collective effort. The section introduces the idea that waste management is a shared civic responsibility and that each person can contribute. Children are asked to observe and report on the cleanliness of their own school surroundings.

tasks.task_text: Observe your surroundings.
How clean are the surroundings of your school?
Do you find waste lying in or outside your classrooms or on the school grounds?

sections.title: Reduce
sections.content_summary: The first rule of waste management is to reduce the amount of waste created. People avoid packaged food and plastic bottles, use cloth bags instead of plastic bags, share old toys and books, and buy only what they need. The more we buy, the more we throw away; the aim is to create as little waste as possible. Children are prompted to draw their locality, identify waste spots, and think of personal ways to reduce waste.

tasks.task_text: Draw or paste a picture of a locality. Identify the places where there is garbage or wastewater or smoke by circling them in red. Try to find out how the waste gets there.
How can you help in reducing waste?

sections.title: Reuse
sections.content_summary: The second principle is to reuse things rather than throwing them away. Traditional practices include making quilts from old sarees and bags from old cloth — IKS practices of upcycling that conserve resources and reduce waste. Reusing single-sided paper, refilling steel or copper bottles, and wrapping gifts in newspapers or old cloth are concrete reuse strategies. Children are shown step-by-step instructions to fold a paper bag using only newspaper with no scissors or glue.

tasks.task_text: How can you REUSE things to reduce waste? You can reuse paper that has been used on one side for rough work. Instead of buying water bottle, reuse a bottle made of a safe material like steel or copper by refilling it. You can make toys or decorative things using old newspapers, old calendars, bottles, boxes, etc.
Create an item using waste material that you can give as a gift to someone.

sections.title: A Place of Pride
sections.content_summary: Some Indian villages and cities have become models of waste management. Silluk village in Arunachal Pradesh is a 'zero waste village' with clean, tree-lined roads and a national award for waste management. Chhota Narena was one of the first villages to become waste-free. Cities like Indore and Mysuru are known for being clean. The Swachh Bharat Mission is referenced as a national programme driving this change.

tasks.task_text: Ask your elders, if there is any village, town or city that they know about, which is known for its cleanliness. You can share the information you learn from your elders in your class.
Find out from your parents or teachers about the Swachh Bharat Mission.

sections.title: Separate Different Types of Waste
sections.content_summary: Waste must be separated into two categories using colour-coded dustbins: the green bin for organic waste (dried leaves, fruit peels, vegetable waste, egg shells) that rots and becomes compost, and the blue bin for recyclable materials (metal, glass, plastic, paper) that can be remade into new objects. Some villages have separate bins for each recyclable material type. Separating and recycling waste keeps surroundings clean and manages waste effectively.

tasks.task_text: Two dustbins are drawn below. Write the names of the waste material which will go into each of them.

sections.title: Keeping our Surroundings Clean
sections.content_summary: The chapter closes with a call to take a personal pledge on National Cleanliness Day (30 January) to keep oneself and one's surroundings clean — not to create too much waste, to manage it, and to dispose of it properly. The guiding motto is: 'Want not, Waste not.' Children list cleaning actions they can take at home and school, and identify and draw cleaning tools used in their home and neighbourhood.

tasks.task_text: List the things that you can do to keep your home and classroom clean?
Make a list of tools you have seen being used in your school or neighbourhood for cleaning.
Draw pictures of these tools in the box given below.
How is waste created?
How can we manage waste?
To avoid use of plastic bags and bottles, look at your home and school closely. List all the items of plastic material and write how these could be replaced by other safe materials.
Name three things in your school and home that you would put in the green dustbin and three things that you would put in the blue dustbin.
Make a poster to show a village or town that is managing its waste well. Give your poster a suitable title.
You can be a clean town and your partner a dirty town. Engage in a short conversation talking about how you feel about being the way you are.

Imagine you are having a birthday party at your home. What are some of the ways in which you can try to have a zero-waste birthday party? For this, first think about what kind of waste may be created in the birthday party and how you can avoid or reduce it.
Have you seen waste in nature? What do you think happens to the waste of animals, dried leaves and so on in a forest?

dual_strand.natural: Waste generation and its environmental consequences (pollution, disease, harm to animals)
dual_strand.human_cultural: Community waste management practices — reduce, reuse, recycle, separate; traditional upcycling (quilts from sarees, cloth bags); civic responsibility and the Swachh Bharat Mission
